title: A Strategic Choice for Cuba's Economy and the Future of US Oil Production
content:
While developing nations face crucial strategic choices about their economic futures, established global industries are also approaching significant turning points. For instance, economic experts propose that a grand bargain could successfully turn around Cuba’s struggling economy. Because Cuba currently imports more than half of its food, Cuban American families are eager to apply modern technologies to revive the nation's once-grand sugar and rum industries. To achieve this recovery, Cuba must decide whether to seek admission into powerful organizations like the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank. These institutions offer vast technical expertise and lending capacities that could drastically transform the island's economic landscape.

At the same time, the global energy sector is facing its own strategic reckoning regarding future oil production. For years, industry analysts have pondered where the next big oil finds will come from to offset declining output from existing fields. While the United States currently supplies more than one-fifth of the world’s oil and gas, production from its densely drilled shale basins cannot grow endlessly. In fact, official government forecasts predict that US oil output will likely peak by 2029 before slowly fading on a gentle downslope. As a result, major oil corporations are now consolidating their operations and searching for new sources of supply to maintain their profits.
